payload.title: Kaido: A Distribution-Market Primitive for
payload.body:
Kaido: A Distribution-Market Primitive for
Continuous Forecasting on Stellar
Arko Roy
1
1
Independent researcher. Email: arkoroy302@gmail.com
Abstract
Existing prediction markets are fragmented.  Users can forecast binary outcomes
across politics, sports, and crypto, but they cannot easily express full probability dis-
tributions, trade continuous beliefs, price uncertainty, or exit with payouts scaled to
forecast accuracy. Real beliefs are not coin flips; they are shapes—“Bitcoin probably
drifts toward ~$68k, with a fat tail toward $75k”; “the election margin is likely +3
to +6, but a blowout isn’t impossible.” We present Kaido, a Stellar-native distribution-
market protocol that turns shaped beliefs into tradable positions. Users submit Gaussian
beliefs (μ,σ) through a two-slider Belief Surface, trade against an L
2
-norm distribution
AMM implemented in Soroban, settle through a tiered oracle framework, and bootstrap
liquidity via BlendTap JIT borrowing from Blend lending pools.
Legal Disclaimer
This White Paper is a proposed technical paper and architecture plan for Kaido v1. It is shared
for feedback and discussion, and should not be read as the final protocol architecture, a product
guarantee, financial advice, or an offer to buy or sell any token or financial instrument. Kaido
involves DeFi, distribution-market AMM accounting, oracle resolution, and smart contracts, all
of which require extensive testing, audits, risk review, and possible design changes before any
production release. Parameters, mechanisms, integrations, and roadmap items described here
may change materially as the protocol is developed.

1

1  Introduction
Prediction markets, as they exist today, ask a single impoverished question: will X happen—yes
or no? That format throws away almost everything a forecaster actually believes. Real beliefs
are not coin flips; they are shapes.
Kaido is a protocol that lets anyone trade those shapes. It is built on the distribution-
market mechanism in Distribution Markets (White, Paradigm, December 2024): an auto-
mated market maker (AMM) not over tokens, but over entire probability distributions, such
that, in an efficient market, the market’s state becomes the crowd’s best collective forecast of a
continuous quantity.
Kaido packages this into two layers:
1. The Distribution Engine—a Soroban (Stellar smart-contract) implementation of a
bounded-loss distribution-market AMM. It is outcome-agnostic: any market that sup-
plies a numeric outcome space, a resolver (oracle), and underwriter liquidity can be created
permissionlessly.
2. The Belief Surface—a consumer-facing UI where users set where they think the
number lands and how confident they are in two slider gestures.  The interface
compiles those into a Gaussian belief (μ,σ), prices it against the market’s current curve,
and shows cost, max payout, and worst case before confirmation.
1.1  The problem with yes/no markets
Binary prediction markets (Polymarket, Kalshi) compress rich beliefs into a single bit. Most
interesting questions have a number as an answer: Bitcoin’s price, election margin, rainfall in
mm, inflation print. Hacking yes/no markets onto these requires dozens of separate markets and
still cannot express how the pieces fit together.
1.2  Distribution markets
A distribution market lets traders submit a shape—a curve over all possible numbers—and
profit based on how close that shape was to what actually happened. Two properties make this
powerful:
• It captures everything you believe at once—not just “up or down” but “where, and how
sure.”
• It rewards honesty about uncertainty via proper scoring rules (Gneiting and Raftery
(2007)).
Table 1 situates Kaido in the mechanism landscape.
2  Distribution Markets: The Mechanism
This section follows Distribution Markets (White, Paradigm, December 2024). The core invari-
ant: the L
2
norm of the position curve, analogous to xy = k in Uniswap.
2

Table 1: Mechanism family tree.
MechanismWhat you bet on   Expressiveness  Money?
Binary prediction marketYES or NO1 bitYes
Categorical marketOne of N buckets  Few bitsYes
Scoring-rule forecastingFull distribution   Whole shape    Usually no
Distribution market (Kaido)  Full distribution   Whole shape   Yes

2.4  Gaussian trades
Each belief is (μ,σ). A Gaussian PDF’s L
2
norm depends only on σ, so shifting μ is cheap;
sharpening (small σ) auto-throttles position size via
λ = k

3  Kaido: System Architecture
Kaido comprises Layer 1 (Soroban contracts), Layer 2 (Belief Surface UI), and an oracle frame-
work.
3.1  Layer 1: Distribution Engine
Soroban contract suite:
• MarketFactory—permissionless create_market(...)  with outcome space, Gaussian
parameterization, k, b, fee bps, Resolver, and trading window.
• DistributionMarket—per-market AMM: collateral, trade(belief, collateral), LP
add/remove, resolution payout.
• BlendAdapter—optional BlendTap JIT borrow spine.
• Registry—indexes markets and resolver trust tiers.
A Kaido market is the tuple:
M = (OutcomeSpace, Parameterization,k,b, fee, Resolver, Window)(3.1)
3.2  Market types
Scalar markets resolve to one final number (BTC close, election margin, CPI). Trajectory
markets resolve to a path x(t) sampled at checkpoints; beliefs are distributions over the path
vector.
3.3  Oracle framework
Table 2: Resolver trust tiers.
Tier  How truth arrivesTrustGood for
T0   On-chain feed (Reflector)Oracle networkCrypto, FX
T1   Attested off-chain reportNamed provider + challenge window  Elections, sports, weather
T2   Optimistic oracle + dispute  Economic bondsLong-tail metrics
T3   Designated resolverNamed partyCommunity/fun markets
4

3.4  BlendTap liquidity bootstrap
On the first trade(), the market JIT-borrows counterparty USDC from a Blend lending pool
(collateral = trader margin).  At claim(), forfeitures repay the borrow.  No protocol-owned
book or seed transaction required.
3.5  Layer 2: Belief Surface
Two sliders (μ center, σ confidence) plus pre-trade quote (cost, worst case, max payout). At
resolution: result card with curve, truth marker, payout, and calibration-scored leaderboards.
3.6  Launch wedge
Day one: “Where does BTC close on Friday?” with Reflector T0 oracle, BlendTap depth,
1 USDC minimum stake, 1% fee. Then permissionless market creation across scalar and trajec-
tory markets.
3.7  Economics
No protocol token at launch; settlement in USDC on Stellar. Fees split between LPs and protocol
treasury.  Value capture: fee share, LP returns, and SDK adoption as Stellar’s distribution-
market primitive.
4  Why Stellar, Why Now
• Micro-stakes need micro-fees. Sub-cent fees and ∼5-second finality make 90-second,
1-USDC markets viable (Soroban documentation).
• Ecosystem fit. Reflector oracles, Soroban contracts, passkey onboarding, and SCF fund-
ing for new financial primitives (SCF Handbook).
• Mechanism gap.  White’s distribution markets (December 2024) have no production
implementation; a 6–12 month window exists before replication on other chains.
5  Worked Examples
Example A—BTC close (scalar, T0). Outcome ∈ [$60k, $80k]; belief μ = $68,200, σ =
$1,400; stake 1 USDC; resolve via Reflector at Friday close.
Example B—US popular-vote margin 2028 (scalar, T1).  Outcome ∈ [−20, +20]
percentage points; capped-Gaussian enabled; certified result via attested adapter with 7-day
challenge window.
Example C—Mumbai rainfall (scalar, T1/T2). Outcome ∈ [0, 300] mm; right-skewed
parameterization in later versions; meteorological service adapter with optimistic fallback.
5

6  Security, Risks, and Mitigations
7  Regulatory Posture
Kaido manages regulatory sensitivity deliberately:
1. Build as a market, not a casino—transparent AMM fees, not house odds.
2. Position as a forecasting platform—crypto is the wedge, not the identity.
3. Geofence sensitive jurisdictions with honest disclosures.
4. Label resolver trust tiers; no false “trustless” claims.
5. No protocol token at launch.
6. Obtain legal opinion before mainnet.
8  Conclusion
Kaido turns shaped probabilistic beliefs about continuous outcomes into tradable, settleable
positions on Stellar.  The construction relies on independently auditable Soroban contracts
whose dependency graph is a strict pipeline: a distribution-market AMM implements the L
2
-
norm invariant, a MarketFactory and Registry enable permissionless market creation, a tiered
resolver framework reports realized outcomes, BlendTap JIT-borrows counterparty liquidity from
Blend pools on the first trade, and the Belief Surface compiles two-slider Gaussian beliefs into
on-chain positions.  We deliberately do not present the protocol as a guaranteed-forecast or
guaranteed-profit product: the realized USDC payout at resolution is determined by the scoring
rule evaluated at the oracle-reported outcome x
0
, which depends on the trader’s submitted belief
curve relative to the market’s aggregate curve and on the trust tier of the resolver.
We have given the implementation-side specification of each layer, focu

sing on the mechanisms
that are specific to Kaido’s execution model: the discrete sphere invariant (Eq. 2.1), the Cauchy–
Schwarz equilibrium mapping (Eq. 2.2), the continuous Hilbert-space invariant (Eq. 2.3), the
6

σ-floor bound that enforces capped peak payout (Eq. 2.5), the λ-scaling rule that throttles
confident beliefs (Eq. 2.6), the always-solvent settlement identity (Eq. 2.4), and the market
tuple that parameterizes permissionless creation (Eq. 3.1). The composition is what matters:
a stack with each layer operating under its own narrow trust assumption and contributing one
financial primitive.
9  References
1. D. White, “Distribution Markets,” Paradigm Whitepaper, 2024. https://www.paradigm.xyz
/2024/12/distribution-markets
2. R. Hanson, “Logarithmic Market Scoring Rules for Modular Combinatorial Information
Aggregation,” 2003.
3. R. Hanson, “Combinatorial Information Market Design,” 2003.
4. T. Gneiting and A. E. Raftery, “Strictly Proper Scoring Rules, Prediction, and Estima-
tion,” Journal of the American Statistical Association, 2007. https://doi.org/10.1198/0162
14506000001437
5. Stellar Development Foundation, “Soroban Documentation,” 2024. https://soroban.stella
r.org
6. Reflector, “Stellar Oracle Network Documentation,” 2024. https://reflector.network/docs
7. Stellar Community Fund, “SCF Handbook,” 2024. https://stellar.gitbook.io/scf-handbook

ulus: distributional forecasting without financial stakes.
